EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved over the Cape Cod Canal and coupled with a strong low level jet to produce a burst of heavy snow over much of Southern New England during the morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed in Pelham by heavy snow and winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in the Great Lakes redeveloped south of New England, spreading snow across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow fell in eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through southern New England from the southwest spreading snow across the area. This was followed by strong warm advection resulting in a precipitation change to freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly six inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.  A brief change to freezing rain resulted in a glaze of ice on top of the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across the Ohio Valley and into southern New England. This low developed a secondary low off the coast of New Jersey. These two low pressure systems resulted in precipitation overspreading the area while warm air moved in aloft. This made precipitation type a challenge with snow, freezing rain, and sleet occurring across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eighth of an inch of ice and 2.6 inches of snow fell in eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across the Ohio Valley off the shore of New Jersey and south of Southern New England, spreading snow across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell in eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure system moved southeast of Nantucket, spreading snow across Southern New England. Snowfall amounts ranged from three inches on Cape Cod to almost twelve inches in southern New Hampshire. This late season storm affected most of the east coast and resulted in hundreds of flight cancellations at Boston's Logan Airport and many car accidents.  In New Hampshire, snowfall amounts ranged from seven to nearly twelve inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to eleven inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through Southern New England coupled with a 50 mph low level jet produced strong gradient winds across much of the area.  Winds remained below advisory criteria (31 mph sustained or 46 mph gusts) but with some trees still partially to fully leafed, these winds did produce some minor damage.||In Pelham, New Hampshire, this minor damage had unfortunate consequences when a strong gust of wind toppled a weakened birch tree onto a 10 year old trick or treater, killing him.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 10 year old boy was killed when a birch tree was blown over by a strong gust of wind.  Photos of the tree shown on local news outlets as well as reports in local newspapers indicated that the tree was at least partially rotted.  ||The closest observations are available eight to nine miles away at either Lawrence Municipal Airport (KLWM), MA and Nashua, Boire Field (KASH), NH.  Observations on the hour either side of the accident recorded wind gusts to 43 mph at KLWM and 21 mph at KASH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across southern New England bringing cold air in at the upper levels and producing showers and thunderstorms. The cold air at the upper levels created conditions conducive to the production of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail fell in Nashua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved through Southern New England, coupling with a warm, tropical airmass to produce showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees along Eaton Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved through Southern New England, coupling with a warm, tropical airmass to produce showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees on the west side of Manchester, downtown Manchester, and on Middle Street were downed by thunderstorm winds.  A tree was downed onto a house in Manchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a moist airmass, resulting in heavy rainfall as well as a couple of isolated severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree on Old Spofford Road was downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a moist airmass, resulting in heavy rainfall as well as a couple of isolated severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees on Route 9 were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved over the Cape Cod Canal and coupled with a strong low level jet to produce a burst of heavy snow over much of Southern New England during the morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by snow and winds in Troy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved over the Cape Cod Canal and coupled with a strong low level jet to produce a burst of heavy snow over much of Southern New England during the morning commute.

A strong low pressure system moved through southern New England from the southwest spreading snow across the area. This was followed by strong warm advection resulting in a precipitation change to freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six and a half inches of snow fell across Cheshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure moved northeast through the Great Lakes and into southern Canada on the 9th.  A secondary low developed off the East Coast and moved rapidly northeast.  The low brought a moderate to heavy snowfall across the state.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches across most of the state with lesser amounts in the Connecticut River Valley.  Along the coast, the snow changed to rain with only an inch or two of accumulation.

An area of low pressure over the mid Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 8th moved to the mid Atlantic States by the morning of the 9th and then well out to sea by the morning of the 10th.  The low brought heavy snow to southern sections of interior Rockingham County where amounts ranged from 5 to 9 inches.  Generally, 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across Merrimack, Sullivan, Belknap, Strafford, and southern Carroll and southern Grafton Counties.  Lesser amounts fell to the north.

An area of low pressure over the southeastern states on the morning of the 1st moved northeast along the coast to just east of the Delmarva Peninsula by the morning of the 2nd and then into the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 3rd.  The storm brought heavy snow to southern part of the state.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 5 inches in the north to almost a foot in sections of Rockingham County.

An area of low pressure moving through the Ohio Valley and a secondary low developing off the southern New England coast combined to produce heavy snow across the state, with a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain in southern areas.  In coastal Rockingham County the mixed precipitation changed to rain for several hours during the evening of the 28th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches in Rockingham and Stratford Counties to 8 to 14 inches across much of central and northern New Hampshire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow fell in western and central Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving out of eastern New York into northern New England produced a moderate to strong mid level jet and good height falls over moderately unstable lower layers. Good speed and some directional shear resulted in rotating cells which evolved into bows and lines. Some large hail was reported but wind damage was the main product of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced nickel sized hail which covered the ground in Milan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a shortwave approaching from the west. A few long lived supercells developed in New York and Canada. These storms moved southeast with the largest and longest lived storm producing EF0 damage in central Orange County Vermont before crossing into Grafton County New Hampshire. There were multiple reports of trees down and large hail associated with this storm as it moved through Grafton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed several trees north of Highway 25a near Wentworth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a pre-frontal trough on the afternoon and evening of July 27th. A couple of storms became severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.75 inch hail in Conway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a pre-frontal trough on the afternoon and evening of July 27th. A couple of storms became severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees, one on a building, on Narrows and Bruster Roads along Lower Suncook Lake in Barnstead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist environment with dewpoints in the low 70s and plenty of sunshine combined to produce showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees on River and Fern Park Roads were downed by thunderstorm winds.  One of these trees blocked River Road and another landed on a house on Fern Park Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Bill developed into a tropical depression over the eastern Atlantic on August 15, 2009.  Bill then strengthened into a hurricane over the next week, passing well to the east of Nantucket, August 22-23.  While Bill passed Southern New England well offshore, he still made quite an impression on the area.  Tropical moisture feeding into the area resulted in heavy rainfall which led to flooding and flash flooding.  High swells churned up by Bill resulted in high surf and coastal flooding along portions of the Southern New England shoreline.  These effects were felt the most by residents of Hillsborough County, New Hampshire, where five inches of rain fell in less than eight hours resulting in significant flooding across portions of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hancock, a house on Middle Road had a flooded basement with the furnace under water.  In Petersborough, two basements were flooded on 7th and Union Streets.  Also, City Avenue was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Bill developed into a tropical depression over the eastern Atlantic on August 15, 2009.  Bill then strengthened into a hurricane over the next week, passing well to the east of Nantucket, August 22-23.  While Bill passed Southern New England well offshore, he still made quite an impression on the area.  Tropical moisture feeding into the area resulted in heavy rainfall which led to flooding and flash flooding.  High swells churned up by Bill resulted in high surf and coastal flooding along portions of the Southern New England shoreline.  These effects were felt the most by residents of Hillsborough County, New Hampshire, where five inches of rain fell in less than eight hours resulting in significant flooding across portions of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dublin and Harkness Roads were both flooded, resulting in road washouts on both.  Dublin Road was able to be re-opened two days later.  Harkness Road was completely washed out and remained closed several days later.  In addition, at least one basement was flooded in Jaffrey on Pine Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist environment with dewpoints in the low 70s and plenty of sunshine combined to produce showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees in the Dummerston Section of Keene were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Bill developed into a tropical depression over the eastern Atlantic on August 15, 2009.  Bill then strengthened into a hurricane over the next week, passing well to the east of Nantucket, August 22-23.  While Bill passed Southern New England well offshore, he still made quite an impression on the area.  Tropical moisture feeding into the area resulted in heavy rainfall which led to flooding and flash flooding.  High swells churned up by Bill resulted in high surf and coastal flooding along portions of the Southern New England shoreline.  These effects were felt the most by residents of Hillsborough County, New Hampshire, where five inches of rain fell in less than eight hours resulting in significant flooding across portions of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A little over five inches of rain fell in eight hours, with most of the rain falling between 3 and 7pm across Hillsborough County.  This resulted in numerous road closures.  Two of these roads were closed as the result of a mudslide and collapsed hillside that spread across the Route 202 North just before its intersection with Route 136 in Peterborough.  In addition, portions of Routes 136 and 101, as well as Ashlee Drive and Sand Hill, Old Dublin, Slab, Gulf, Old Greenfield, Forest, Cavender, County, Old Bennington and Hopkins Roads were undermined, flooded, and/or closed.  The Peterborough Fire Department rescued seven people who were trapped in their cars on Old Greenfield Road.  Route 101 east flooded with two to three inches of flowing water, which flooded and eroded yards nearby.  Floodwaters were forceful enough that they moved a guardrail on Route 136.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers associated with a cold front produced 2 to 3 inches of rain which lead to minor flooding of the Pemigewasset River in Woodstock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers associated with a cold front produced 2 to 3 inches of rain resulting in minor flooding on the Pemigewasset River at Woodstock, (flood stage 9.0 ft), which crested at 9.13 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm brought high winds and rain to much of Southern New England. These winds resulted in tree damage across much of southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Winding Road in Hancock, Poor Farm Road in Francestown, and in Temple.  A piece of sheet metal on the roof of an auto body shop in Brookline was blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm brought high winds and rain to much of Southern New England. These winds resulted in tree damage across much of southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Jaffrey Municipal-Silver Ranch Airport (KAFN) recorded wind gusts of 51 mph.  In addition, trees and wires were downed on several roads throughout Cheshire County, including the communities of Richmond, Chesterfield, and Jaffrey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure intensified as it slowly moved up the coast from New York City to the Gulf of Maine. Low lying roads were flooded in Hampton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An are of low pressure in the Gulf of Maine produced minor coastal flooding in Hampton, New Hampshire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developing east of Cape Cod brought heavy rain to the entire State...which mixed with and then changed to heavy snow in the higher elevations of Coos County where as much as 19 inches fell from the two-day event.  The higher elevations of northern Grafton and Carroll Counties also saw a change to snow with a few locations getting significant snow.

A low pressure system moved across the Ohio Valley off the shore of New Jersey and south of Southern New England, spreading snow across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly seven inches of snow fell across Cheshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across the Ohio Valley off the shore of New Jersey and south of Southern New England, spreading snow across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight and a half inches of snow fell across western and central Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front associated with of low pressure over the Great Lakes approached the region from the southwest on the morning of December 9th. A secondary area of low pressure developed on the front in the afternoon and tracked into the Gulf of Maine. Strong winds associated with this system downed several trees in Coos County, one of which landed on a home in Groveton. Several trees were also blown down in Whitefield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed a large pine tree on a home in Groveton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure moving through the Gulf of Maine coincided with the highest astronomical tides of the month to produce coastal flooding along the New Hampshire coast. Strong southeast winds produced a storm surge of 1.44 ft and near shore waves of 7 to 10 ft. In Hampton, the storm tide produced flooding a full two hours after high tide, closing roads in the town near Ashworth Avenue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure moving through the Gulf of Maine and astronomically high tides produced coastal flooding in Hampton. Roads were closed in town near Ashworth Street due to the high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, on the order of 1 to 2 inches, combined with melting snow to produce minor flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt caused minor flooding on the Suncook River, (flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested at 7.48 ft.

Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 8 to 15 across most of the state with 4 to 8 inches in the Connecticut River Valley and in Coos County.

High astronomical tides combined with low pressure which exited off the southern New England coastline to produce minor flooding on Route 1 in Hampton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with the circulation around an area low pressure to produce minor coastal flooding in Hampton, New Hampshire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure system moved southeast of Nantucket, spreading snow across Southern New England. Snowfall amounts ranged from three inches on Cape Cod to almost twelve inches in southern New Hampshire. This late season storm affected most of the east coast and resulted in hundreds of flight cancellations at Boston's Logan Airport and many car accidents.  In New Hampshire, snowfall amounts ranged from seven to nearly twelve inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to twelve inches of snow fell across Cheshire County.

In New Hampshire, snowfall amounts ranged from seven to nearly twelve inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to thirteen inches of snow fell across western and central Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure in the mid-Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 21st moved northeast through the Great Lakes by the morning of the 22nd.  A secondary low developed off the mid-Atlantic coast during the day on the 22nd and then moved northeast along the coast to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 23rd.  The combination of the two systems brought heavy snow to the state, except in Rockingham County where much of the precipitation fell as rain.  Snowfall amounts ranged from about 3 inches in Rockingham County to more than a foot across eastern Carroll County.  Most of the state received between 6 and 12 inches of snow.

An area of low pressure moved from the mid-Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 18th to southern Ontario by the morning of the 19th.  A secondary low developed in the Gulf of Maine during the day on the 19th and moved to New Brunswick by the morning of the 20th.  The combination of low pressure systems brought heavy snow to most of the state with 6 to 12 inches of accumulation being reported.  Southern areas saw the snow mix with rain.

Low pressure moved out of the Ohio River Valley resulting in .75 to 1.00 inch of rain causing minor flooding on the Suncook River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in minor flooding on the Suncook River at Chichester(flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested at 7.5 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving out of the Ohio River Valley resulted in minor flooding on the Suncook River at Chichester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on the Suncook River at Chichester(flood stage 7.0 ft.) which crested at 7.7 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving out of eastern New York into northern New England produced a moderate to strong mid level jet and good height falls over moderately unstable lower layers. Good speed and some directional shear resulted in rotating cells which evolved into bows and lines. Some large hail was reported but wind damage was the main product of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Crossmill Road near Northfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving out of eastern New York into northern New England produced a moderate to strong mid level jet and good height falls over moderately unstable lower layers. Good speed and some directional shear resulted in rotating cells which evolved into bows and lines. Some large hail was reported but wind damage was the main product of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires just west of Laconia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced mainly large hail in southeast New Hampshire during the afternoon of June 26th. Hail up to half dollar size was reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Sanbornville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across Southern New England as an upper level low moved across the area.  Weak low level winds resulted in these storms moving very slowly, if at all.  Rainfall rates of up to four inches per hour produced flash flooding, resulting in street closures and basement flooding at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail fell in Brookline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced mainly large hail in southeast New Hampshire during the afternoon of June 26th. Hail up to half dollar size was reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped half dollar size hail near Deerfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced mainly large hail in southeast New Hampshire during the afternoon of June 26th. Hail up to half dollar size was reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .88 inch hail in Alton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced mainly large hail in southeast New Hampshire during the afternoon of June 26th. Hail up to half dollar size was reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Strafford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across southern New England bringing cold air in at the upper levels and producing showers and thunderstorms. The cold air at the upper levels created conditions conducive to the production of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail fell in Hudson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced up to 3 inches of rain in 2 hours causing flash flooding in Bow and Hooksett New Hampshire. Total damages reached $100,000.00.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding on small rivers and streams in the Hooksett area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced up to 3 inches of rain in a short period of time causing flash flooding in the Belmont area of Belknap County. Damages were estimated at $50,000.00.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Belmont area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced up to 3 inches of rain in 2 hours causing flash flooding in Bow and Hooksett New Hampshire. Total damages reached $100,000.00.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding on small rivers and streams in the Bow area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak cold front and short wave approaching from the Great Lakes. Heating in the morning provided a marginally unstable environment. Shear was expected to be relatively weak, and low wet bulb zero values suggested marginally severe hail was possible. Several  severe thunderstorms did produce large hail in southeastern New Hampshire..
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Tilton.

Several  severe thunderstorms did produce large hail in southeastern New Hampshire..
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Franklin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a shortwave approaching from the west. A few long lived supercells developed in New York and Canada. These storms moved southeast with the largest and longest lived storm producing EF0 damage in central Orange County Vermont before crossing into Grafton County New Hampshire. There were multiple reports of trees down and large hail associated with this storm as it moved through Grafton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees and power lines near Lake Tarleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm downed trees in Belknap County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on power lines on Horn Road in Belmont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms produced 3 to 4 inches of rain, (radar estimated), in a 3 hour period. This caused flash flooding in Thornton and Livermore. Both Tripoli Road and Lebrecque Road were washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in the town of Livermore washing out several roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms produced 3 to 4 inches of rain, (radar estimated), in a 3 hour period. This caused flash flooding in Thornton and Livermore. Both Tripoli Road and Lebrecque Road were washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Thornton washing out several roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed in the warm sector characterized by moderate low-level shear and low LCLs. Wind damage was the most common form of severe weather, however a few cells showed extremely high cores and low-level rotation which produced 1 report of large hail and numerous reports of heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail near Danville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed in the warm sector characterized by moderate low-level shear and low LCLs. Wind damage was the most common form of severe weather, however a few cells showed extremely high cores and low-level rotation which produced 1 report of large hail and numerous reports of heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed large branches in Littleton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees across Goose Pond Road in Canaan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a large pine tree near the Whitaker Suite Building in North Conway and caused damage to electrical equipment, phone lines, afire alarm system and about 13 televisions in buildings in the nearby area.
